name: Baldur
description: Baldur, also spelled Baldr, is a prominent figure in Norse mythology, revered as the god of light, beauty, joy, and purity. He is the son of Odin, the Allfather, and Frigg, the goddess of marriage and motherhood. Baldur's character is often depicted as exceptionally fair and radiant, embodying ideal masculine beauty and grace. His presence brings light and joy, and he is generally considered the most beloved of the gods. His tragic death at the hands of his blind brother, Höðr, manipulated by the trickster god Loki, is a pivotal event in Norse mythology, foreshadowing the coming of Ragnarök, the twilight of the gods. Baldur's demise plunges the world into darkness and despair, symbolizing the loss of innocence and the inevitability of fate. His anticipated return after Ragnarök signifies hope and the promise of a renewed world.
subtitle: The Shining God

keyMyths.title: Baldur's Dreams and Frigg's Oath
keyMyths.description: Baldur begins to have prophetic dreams of his own death, causing great distress among the gods. To protect him, his mother Frigg extracts an oath from every thing in existence – plants, animals, stones, diseases, and weapons – promising not to harm Baldur. This makes Baldur seemingly invulnerable, and the gods amuse themselves by throwing objects at him, which bounce harmlessly off. This myth showcases Frigg's devotion and foretells the tragedy to come.

keyMyths.title: Baldur's Death
keyMyths.description: Loki, envious of Baldur's invulnerability and popularity, discovers that Frigg overlooked the mistletoe, considering it too young and harmless to require an oath. Loki fashions a dart from mistletoe and tricks Höðr, Baldur's blind brother, into throwing it at him. The mistletoe pierces Baldur, killing him instantly. This event is a turning point in Norse mythology, marking the beginning of the events leading to Ragnarök. The grief of the gods is immense, and the world is plunged into sorrow.

keyMyths.title: Hermod's Journey to Hel
keyMyths.description: Odin sends Hermod, Baldur's brother, on a perilous journey to Hel, the realm of the dead, to plead with Hel, the goddess of the underworld, for Baldur's return. Hel agrees to release Baldur only if all things in the world weep for him. While nearly everything weeps, Loki, disguised as a giantess named Þökk, refuses to weep, thus preventing Baldur's return from the underworld. This myth highlights the importance of fate and the inevitability of death in Norse cosmology.

keyMyths.title: Baldur's Return After Ragnarök
keyMyths.description: Despite his death, Baldur is prophesied to return after Ragnarök, the destruction and rebirth of the world. Along with his brother Höðr, who is also resurrected, he will rule over a new and peaceful world, free from strife and sorrow. This signifies hope and renewal after destruction, a recurring theme in Norse mythology. Baldur's return represents the triumph of good over evil and the cyclical nature of time.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Baldur's life, death, and role in Ragnarök.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Baldur's life, death, and role in Ragnarök.

sources.author: Hilda Ellis Davidson
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology, offering insightful analysis of Baldur's significance and symbolism.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Norse mythology, offering insightful analysis of Baldur's significance and symbolism.

sources.work: Norse Mythology: A Guide to the Gods, Heroes, Rituals, and Beliefs
